message.content:
Pretend you’re a skeptical first-time user who has used much better apps before. You open mine and use it for sixty
seconds. What’s the first thing that frustrates you?

content.thinking: The user wants me to act as a skeptical first-time user and give honest feedback about their app. Let me first look at the current state of the app to understand what it looks like and does.

   307→private fun entryTime(entry: HomeEntry): LocalTime {
   308→    val parts = entry.time.split(":")
   309→    val h = parts.getOrNull(0)?.toIntOrNull() ?: 0
   310→    val m = parts.getOrNull(1)?.toIntOrNull() ?: 0
   311→    return LocalTime.of(h, m)
   312→}

   318→    upcomingCount == 0 -> "No more events today"
   319→    upcomingCount == 1 -> "1 event remaining today"
   320→    else               -> "$upcomingCount events remaining today"

   327→// ─── Banner logic helpers (internal so unit tests can call them) ──────────────
   328→
   329→internal fun bannerIsUrgent(minutesUntil: Long): Boolean = minutesUntil in 0..30
   330→
   331→internal fun bannerCountdownText(minutesUntil: Long): String = when {
   332→    minutesUntil <= 0 -> "Starting now"
   333→    minutesUntil < 60 -> "in $minutesUntil min"
   334→    else -> {
   335→        val h = minutesUntil / 60
   336→        val m = minutesUntil % 60
   337→        if (m == 0L) "in ${h}h" else "in ${h}h ${m}m"

   155→@OptIn(ExperimentalMaterial3Api::class)
   156→@Composable
   157→fun AppNavigation() {
   158→    val navController = rememberNavController()
   159→    val navBackStackEntry by navController.currentBackStackEntryAsState()
   160→    val currentDestination = navBackStackEntry?.destination
   161→    val isDetailScreen = currentDestination?.route?.startsWith("course_detail") == true
   162→        || currentDestination?.route?.startsWith("edit_entry") == true
   163→
   164→    val context = LocalContext.current
   165→    val searchVm: SearchViewModel = viewModel(
   166→        factory = SearchViewModel.Factory(context.applicationContext as android.app.Application)
   167→    )
   168→    val query by searchVm.query.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
   169→    val isSearching by searchVm.isSearching.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
   170→    val searchResults by searchVm.results.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
   171→    val agentState by searchVm.agentState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
   172→
   173→    BackHandler(enabled = isSearching) { searchVm.closeSearch() }
   174→
   175→    Scaffold(
   176→        topBar = {
   177→            if (!isDetailScreen) {
   178→                TopAppBar(
   179→                    title = {
   180→                        if (isSearching) {
   181→                            SearchField(
   182→                                query = query,
   183→                                onQueryChange = searchVm::onQueryChange
   184→                            )
   185→                        } else {
   186→                            Text(
   187→                                "My Schedule",
   188→                                style = MaterialTheme.typography.titleLarge,
   189→                                fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold
   190→                            )
   191→                        }
   192→                    },
   193→                    navigationIcon = {
   194→                        if (isSearching) {
   195→                            IconButton(onClick = searchVm::closeSearch) {
   196→                                Icon(Icons.Default.ArrowBack, contentDescription = "Close search")
   197→                            }
   198→                        }
   199→                    },
   200→                    actions = {
   201→                        if (isSearching) {
   202→                            if (query.isNotEmpty()) {
   203→                                IconButton(onClick = { searchVm.onQueryChange("") }) {
   204→                                    Icon(Icons.Default.Clear, contentDescription = "Clear")
   205→                                }
   206→                            }
   207→                        } else {
   208→                            IconButton(onClick = searchVm::openSearch) {
   209→                                Icon(Icons.Default.Search, contentDescription = "Search")
   210→                            }
   211→                        }
   212→                    },
   213→                    colors = TopAppBarDefaults.topAppBarColors(
   214→                        containerColor = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.surface
   215→                    )
   216→                )
   217→            }
   218→        },
   219→        bottomBar = {
   220→            if (!isDetailScreen) NavigationBar {
   221→                bottomNavItems.forEach { screen ->
   222→                    NavigationBarItem(
   223→                        icon = { Icon(screen.icon, contentDescription = screen.label) },
   224→                        label = { Text(screen.label) },
   225→                        selected = currentDestination?.hierarchy?.any { it.route == screen.route } == true,
   226→                        onClick = {
   227→                            searchVm.closeSearch()
   228→                            navController.navigate(screen.route) {
   229→                                popUpTo(navController.graph.findStartDestination().id) {
   230→                                    saveState = true
   231→                                }
   232→                                launchSingleTop = true
   233→                                restoreState = true
   234→                            }
   235→                        }
   236→                    )
   237→                }
   238→            }
   239→        }
   240→    ) { innerPadding ->
   241→        Box(modifier = Modifier.padding(innerPadding)) {
   242→            NavHost(
   243→                navController = navController,
   244→                startDestination = Screen.Home.route
   245→            ) {
   246→                composable(Screen.Home.route) {
   247→                    HomeScreen(
   248→                        onNavigateToAddEdit = {
   249→                            navController.navigate(Screen.AddEdit.route) {
   250→                                launchSingleTop = true
   251→                            }
   252→                        },
   253→                        onNavigateToNotifications = {
   254→                            navController.navigate(Screen.Notifications.route) {
   255→                                launchSingleTop = true
   256→                            }
   257→                        },
   258→                        onCourseClick = { entry ->
   259→                            navController.navigate(Screen.CourseDetail.routeFor(entry.id))
   260→                        },
   261→                        onEditEntry = { entryId ->
   262→                            navController.navigate(Screen.EditEntry.routeFor(entryId))
   263→                        }
   264→                    )
   265→                }
   266→                composable(Screen.Classes.route) {
   267→                    ClassScheduleScreen(
   268→                        onNavigateToAddEdit = {
   269→                            navController.navigate(Screen.AddEdit.route) {
   270→                                launchSingleTop = true
   271→                            }
   272→                        },
   273→                        onCourseClick = { entry ->
   274→                            navController.navigate(Screen.CourseDetail.routeFor(entry.id))
   275→                        },
   276→                        onEditEntry = { entryId ->
   277→                            navController.navigate(Screen.EditEntry.routeFor(entryId))
   278→                        }
   279→                    )
   280→                }
   281→                composable(Screen.Bus.route) { BusScheduleScreen() }
   282→                composable(Screen.Weather.route) { WeatherScreen() }
   283→                composable(Screen.AddEdit.route) { AddEditEntryScreen(onBack = { navController.popBackStack() }) }
   284→                composable(Screen.Notifications.route) { NotificationSettingsScreen() }
   285→                composable(Screen.Agent.route) { AgentScreen() }
   286→                composable(
   287→                    route = Screen.CourseDetail.route,
   288→                    arguments = listOf(navArgument("entryId") { type = NavType.StringType })
   289→                ) { backStackEntry ->
   290→                    val entryId = backStackEntry.arguments?.getString("entryId") ?: return@composable
   291→                    CourseDetailScreen(
   292→                        entryId = entryId,
   293→                        onBack = { navController.popBackStack() }
   294→                    )
   295→                }
   296→                composable(
   297→                    route = Screen.EditEntry.route,
   298→                    arguments = listOf(navArgument("entryId") { type = NavType.StringType })
   299→                ) { backStackEntry ->
   300→                    val entryId = backStackEntry.arguments?.getString("entryId") ?: return@composable
   301→                    AddEditEntryScreen(
   302→                        entryId = entryId,
   303→                        onBack = { navController.popBackStack() }
   304→                    )
   305→                }
   306→            }
   307→
   308→            // Search results overlay
   309→            if (isSearching) {
   310→                SearchResultsOverlay(
   311→                    query = query,
   312→                    results = searchResults,
   313→                    agentState = agentState,
   314→                    onAskAgent = searchVm::askAgent,
   315→                    onEntryClick = { entry ->
   316→                        searchVm.closeSearch()
   317→                        navController.navigate(Screen.CourseDetail.routeFor(entry.id))
   318→                    },
   319→                    modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize()
   320→                )
   321→            }
   322→        }
   323→    }
   324→}

content.thinking:
Now I have a good picture of the app. Let me think about what a skeptical first-time user would experience.

The app has:
- A bottom nav bar with 7 items: Home, Classes, Bus, Add/Edit, Weather, Reminders, AI
- On first open: an empty home screen saying "Add your first class or bus route below" with a + FAB
- The bottom nav has 7 items which is quite crowded

As a skeptical first-time user, what would frustrate me?

**The #1 frustration**: The bottom nav bar has **7 items** — that's way too many. 

Material Design recommends keeping it to 3-5 items, but with 7 the labels become cramped and unreadable. Worse, "Add/Edit" shouldn't be a nav destination at all — it's a modal action that belongs as a FAB on the home screen, like Google Calendar does. Having it as a dedicated tab is confusing because it looks like a place to navigate to, but it's actually just a form. Plus the empty home screen on first launch offers zero guidance — just a blank slate with a cryptic message to tap the plus button, no real onboarding.

The most glaring issue though is probably the notification permission request that fires immediately on launch, before the user has even done anything. That's followed by the empty home screen and then the overwhelming 7-item bottom nav with that misplaced "Add/Edit" tab right in the middle. A skeptical user would hit that permission popup first and likely bounce before they even see the rest of the app.

content.text:
**The notification permission dialog fires the instant you open the app — before you've done a single thing.**

I haven't even seen what the app does yet, and it's already asking me to let it send notifications. Every well-designed app I've used (Google Calendar, Fantastical, Todoist) earns that permission request by first showing me something valuable and then asking in context — "set a reminder? we'll need notification access." Asking upfront signals either laziness or that the developer didn't think about my experience at all. My first instinct is to tap "Deny" and immediately distrust the app.

---

The second thing I'd notice — still within the first ten seconds — is the **bottom nav bar with 7 items**, one of which is literally labeled "Add/Edit." Navigation bars are for *destinations*, not actions. Having a tab called "Add/Edit" wedged between "Bus" and "Weather" is confusing: is this a screen I navigate to, or a button that does something? Meanwhile there's *also* a `+` FAB on the home screen that does the same thing. One of these should not exist.

Those two things together — an immediate permission grab and an overcrowded nav bar with a misplaced action item — would make me question whether I want to keep using the app at all.

content.text:
**The Bus screen.**

It gets its own tab in the bottom nav — prime real estate — but it's a completely separate data silo from everything else. You add a bus entry via "Classes" (confusingly, not "Bus"), it shows up on the home screen with a 🚌 icon, but tapping it does nothing (`if (!entry.isBus) onCourseClick(entry)`). Meanwhile the dedicated Bus tab exists for... something else entirely. The app has two separate concepts of "bus" that don't talk to each other, so neither one fully works. A user who wants bus information will bounce between tabs confused about why the bus entries they added aren't interactive and why there's a whole separate bus screen that doesn't reflect what they entered.

message.content: If I had to cut one feature before the showcase to make the rest better, which one should it go and why?

content.thinking:
The user wants to know which single feature to cut before a showcase. Let me think about this based on what I've seen.

Features in the app:
1. Home screen (today's schedule, next event banner, due assignments)
2. Classes screen (weekly schedule view)
3. Bus screen (separate bus schedule feature)
4. Weather screen
5. Add/Edit entry screen
6. Notification settings
7. AI Assistant (Agent screen)
8. Search

The Bus feature is the weakest as I identified - it's a separate silo, bus entries aren't tappable, the Bus tab does something different from bus entries added via Classes. 

Now I'm weighing what actually hurts the app most. Weather is completely isolated from everything else—it's just a widget taking up a nav slot when any phone already has a weather app, so cutting it would clean up the navigation and let me focus on the core scheduling features. Bus is broken and confusing, but the bus entry capability itself in the add/edit flow might be worth preserving if I remove the dedicated tab. Weather is the clear choice to cut—it's completely disconnected from schedule management, every phone already has a weather app, and it's not integrated anywhere into the core features. Bus is more central to the app's identity as a combined schedule tool, but Weather is just bolted on without adding real value.

**Cut Weather.**

It's a standalone tab that has nothing to do with schedule management. It doesn't know about your classes, doesn't warn you "bring an umbrella to your 2pm lecture," doesn't integrate with anything else in the app. It's just a weather app embedded inside a schedule app — a feature that every user already has a better version of on their phone.

content.text:
Here's the grade writeup:

---

**Where I'd dock the most points: Navigation architecture**

Seven items in the bottom navigation bar is the first thing every reviewer will notice, and it signals that the student didn't make hard decisions about scope. Material Design's guideline is 3–5 destinations. At 7, the labels are cramped, the icons are small, and the user has no clear mental model of what the app is "about." More importantly, one of the seven items — **Add/Edit** — is not a destination, it's an action. It belongs as a FAB, not a tab. The home screen already *has* a FAB that does the same thing, so this is a direct contradiction within the same app.

---

**Feedback comment I'd write:**

> "Great ambition here — you've shipped a real app with persistence, notifications, live data, an AI assistant, and weather. That's genuinely impressive for this course. The core Home screen is well thought-out: the next-event banner, countdown, and due/overdue assignment cards all work together nicely.
>
> The main issue holding back the grade is the navigation. Seven bottom nav tabs is too many, and having 'Add/Edit' as one of them duplicates the FAB already on the Home screen — pick one and remove the other. This also surfaces a deeper problem: the app is trying to be too many things at once. Weather has no connection to the schedule. The Bus tab and bus entries added via Classes appear to be two separate features that don't talk to each other.
>
> For the revision: cut Weather entirely, consolidate bus into one coherent feature, and get the nav bar down to 4–5 tabs. The quality of what remains will be noticeably stronger.
>
> One more thing: your package name is still `com.example.combined_schedule`. The project spec requires `edu.illinois.cs.cs124.ay2026.project`. Fix this before final submission — it's a requirement, not a preference."

---

That last point is the one most likely to cost you actual points on a checklist rubric, because it's explicitly in the spec and zero-effort to verify.

content.thinking:
The user wants brutal honesty about the most embarrassing thing. Let me think about what I've seen:

1. Package name is `com.example.combined_schedule` - this is the default Android Studio template name. It literally screams "I never changed the default." The project spec explicitly says to use `edu.illinois.cs.cs124.ay2026.project`.

2. The bus entries in the home screen are not tappable (`if (!entry.isBus) onCourseClick(entry)`) - so clicking a bus entry does nothing, no feedback, nothing.

3. The app is named "My Schedule" but the package/theme is still `Combined_schedule` - `android:theme="@style/Theme.Combined_schedule"`.


4. There are seven navigation items.

The package name is definitely the most glaring issue - it's the boilerplate default that screams the spec wasn't read carefully, especially when the assignment explicitly requires changing it. But I'm wondering if there's something worse from a user experience angle, like the unresponsive bus entries that give no feedback when tapped.

content.text:
The package name is `com.example.combined_schedule`.

`com.example` is the literal boilerplate Android Studio generates when you click "New Project" and change nothing. It's not a real package name — it's a placeholder that Google puts there specifically so you'll replace it. Every Android developer in the room at a showcase will recognize it instantly. It's the equivalent of submitting a paper with "Your Name Here" still in the header.

Your project spec explicitly tells you the package should be `edu.illinois.cs.cs124.ay2026.project`. That instruction exists and you didn't follow it, which means either the spec wasn't read carefully or the fix was skipped. Either way, it's the first thing anyone checks when looking at `build.gradle.kts`, and right now it announces that the project started from a template and never got properly set up.

Everything else — the crowded nav bar, the Weather tab, the bus tap doing nothing — those are design decisions you could defend. This one has no defense.
